seoTitle: How to Enter a Non-Taxable IHSS W-2 in TurboTax
metaDescription: A clear walkthrough for entering a non-taxable IHSS W-2 in TurboTax for 2026, so caregivers can file accurately without overpaying or triggering an audit.
intro: In-Home Supportive Services (IHSS) wages can qualify as non-taxable income under certain IRS rules, but entering them correctly in TurboTax takes a bit of care. This guide explains how to input a non-taxable IHSS W-2 so your return reflects your actual tax liability.

steps.heading: Understand why the W-2 is non-taxable
steps.body: Certain IHSS caregivers who live with the person they care for can qualify to exclude those wages from federal taxable income under IRS Notice 2014-7, even though a W-2 was issued.

steps.heading: Start entering the W-2 as usual
steps.body: Enter the W-2 into TurboTax the same way you would any other W-2, using the figures exactly as they appear on the form.

steps.heading: Apply the non-taxable adjustment
steps.body: Follow TurboTax's prompts for IHSS or difficulty-of-care payments to back out the taxable amount, which offsets the income shown on the W-2 so it isn't taxed.

steps.heading: Verify the adjustment appears on your return
steps.body: Check that the offsetting entry shows up correctly and that your taxable income reflects the exclusion rather than the full W-2 amount.

steps.heading: Keep documentation on hand
steps.body: Retain records supporting your eligibility for the exclusion in case the IRS or TurboTax's review flags the entry.

faq.question: Do I still need to report the W-2 even if the income isn't taxable?
faq.answer: Yes, you still enter the W-2 in TurboTax and then apply the appropriate exclusion so the income is properly backed out rather than left off your return entirely.

faq.question: Who qualifies for the non-taxable IHSS exclusion?
faq.answer: Caregivers who live in the same home as the person receiving care generally qualify, based on IRS guidance for difficulty-of-care payments.

faq.question: Will this affect my eligibility for tax credits like the EITC?
faq.answer: It can, since some credits allow you to choose whether to include or exclude this income for calculation purposes. Review how TurboTax handles the credit calculation after making the entry.

faq.question: What if TurboTax still shows the income as taxable?
faq.answer: Go back through the IHSS or Notice 2014-7 entry screens to confirm the exclusion was applied correctly, since a missed step can leave the wages taxed.
